title: Witness-Certified Coalgebraic Compression for Finite-Domain TLA+ Model Checking

reviewers.name: Yuan Cheng
reviewers.key_strength: The Merkle-hashed witness scheme provides auditable integrity for bisimulation relations with O(log n) membership proofs.
reviewers.key_weakness: No probabilistic analysis of the bounded conformance tester's coverage or the statistical power of differential testing against TLC.

reviewers.name: Aniruddha Sinha
reviewers.key_strength: On-the-fly quotient construction interleaving exploration and learning avoids materializing bisimulation-equivalent states, a qualitative improvement over TLC's exhaustive enumeration.
reviewers.key_weakness: The bounded conformance tester provides no formal completeness guarantee for liveness preservation under fairness, weakening the T-Fair coherence claim.

reviewers.name: Lila Zhang
reviewers.key_strength: The coalgebraic Myhill-Nerode theorem guarantees existence and uniqueness of the minimal quotient for finite-domain TLA-lite specifications.
reviewers.key_weakness: Missed opportunity to integrate SMT-backed membership oracles for the L* learner, which would avoid materializing the concrete transition system.

reviewers.name: Sara Roy
reviewers.key_strength: The Merkle witness scheme addresses a genuine ML-safety gap: auditable verification artifacts for deployment in safety-critical pipelines.
reviewers.key_weakness: No learning-augmented components beyond classical L*; no evaluation of learning curves, sample efficiency, or neural-guided counterexample generation.

reviewers.name: Joseph S. Chang
reviewers.key_strength: The T-Fair coherence condition is a genuine proof-theoretic contribution establishing that stuttering closure distributes over fairness acceptance pairs.
reviewers.key_weakness: The Myhill-Nerode quotient size bound is tautological; a structural bound relating quotient size to specification parameters would be more informative.

consensus_strengths: Novel coalgebraic endofunctor F(X) incorporating both stuttering closure and TLA+-style fairness is a genuine theoretical advance over Beohar-Küpper (2017).
On-the-fly quotient construction via L*-style learning provides automatic bisimulation compression without manual symmetry annotations.
Merkle-hashed bisimulation witness enables independent third-party verification of model checking results.
Carefully scoped TLA-lite fragment captures all standard distributed protocol benchmarks while ensuring finite-domain decidability.
consensus_weaknesses: The bounded conformance tester lacks formal completeness guarantees, undermining the 'certified' claim for liveness properties.
No integration with SMT solvers despite Apalache's existence—a hybrid symbolic-coalgebraic approach could be significantly more powerful.
Benchmark selection is conservative; TLC already handles the proposed benchmarks (Two-Phase Commit ≤7, Paxos-3) in reasonable time.
No machine-checkable proof artifacts (Lean/Coq); the TCB includes the full Rust implementation beyond the witness verifier.
Learning aspects are underexplored: no analysis of convergence curves, sample efficiency, or modern neural-guided counterexample generation.
actionable_recommendations: Implement a symbolic equivalence oracle using SMT queries to avoid bounded-depth testing limitations and leverage TLA-lite's finite-domain decidability.
Add at least one benchmark where TLC genuinely struggles (e.g., Raft-5, Byzantine protocols) to demonstrate practical compression benefits.
Formalize the bisimulation closure conditions in Lean 4 to reduce TCB to the proof assistant kernel plus the ~3-5K LoC witness verifier.
Include learning curve analysis in the evaluation: plot quotient accuracy vs. number of membership queries to characterize practical convergence.
Provide a structural upper bound on quotient size as a function of specification parameters (variable count, domain sizes, fairness constraint count).
